planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Gold Dust Aucuba is a female cultivar — plant a male aucuba nearby for the red berries that develop in fall and persist through winter. 'Variegata' is female; males include 'Mr. Goldstrike' and other Aucuba japonica male selections. Plant in consistent shade — sun causes unattractive yellowing and leaf scorch. Aucuba tolerates the heavy shade under large evergreen trees, along north-facing walls, and beneath dense canopies where most other shrubs fail. Excellent salt and pollution tolerance makes it useful in difficult urban situations.

directions: Plant in deep shade to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Gold Dust Aucuba is one of the most shade-tolerant large shrubs available, thriving in conditions where direct sunlight never reaches and where most ornamental shrubs slowly decline; Gold Dust is the classic variegated cultivar of Japanese Aucuba (Aucuba japonica), producing large, glossy, dark green leaves heavily speckled and splashed with bright gold and yellow markings — a bold, striking foliage pattern that provides strong ornamental interest in the deep shade conditions where the plant grows best. The gold-speckled leaves literally brighten dark shade garden areas, reflecting and amplifying available light in a way that creates a visual sense of brightness even under dense tree canopy. The combination of large, lustrous, gold-variegated foliage with the tolerance to thrive in genuinely deep shade — not just the 'partial shade' that many 'shade plants' actually require — makes Gold Dust Aucuba one of the most useful and irreplaceable shrubs for the specific problem of planting in dense shade under established trees, along north-facing walls, or in any situation where direct sunlight is effectively absent. Pollinated female plants produce clusters of bright red to scarlet berries that develop in fall and persist through winter, adding seasonal fruit color to the year-round foliage interest.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Aucuba japonica for Southern Shade Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Shade Shrubs for the Gulf South
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Evergreen Shrubs for Problem Shaded Sites

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–May or September–October. Foxtail Fern grows from tuberous roots that store moisture, making it more drought tolerant than its lush appearance suggests. In zone 9 it is evergreen to semi-evergreen, surviving mild winters with little damage; hard freezes (below 28°F) will damage or kill the above-ground stems, but the tuberous roots typically survive and resprout. Small white flowers and red berries form on established plants — the berries are mildly toxic if ingested. Despite the common name, this is not a true fern but an asparagus relative with needle-like cladodes.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Foxtail Fern adapts to a wide range of light conditions but produces its densest, most architectural stems in consistent partial shade; Myers Foxtail Fern is distinguished from the better-known Sprengeri Fern (Asparagus densiflorus 'Sprengeri') by its dramatically different growth habit — instead of the loose, trailing, cascading stems of Sprengeri, Myers produces dense, upright, bottlebrush-like stems 12–18 inches long that radiate symmetrically from the center like the tail of a fox, creating an architectural, structural plant form that is highly effective as a container plant, border specimen, and cut greenery. The precise, uniform, densely needled stems are prized by florists and cut flower arrangers for their long vase life, unique texture, and the soft, fine-needle effect that provides exceptional filler material in arrangements. As a landscape plant in zone 9, Foxtail Fern's dense, mounding habit and tolerance for partial shade and occasional drought make it an effective and low-maintenance ground cover or border edging plant. The evergreen to semi-evergreen foliage in Gulf South winters and the container culture versatility (it grows excellently in large pots) add to its practical utility.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asparagus densiflorus 'Myers' Landscape Use
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Asparagus for Gulf Coast Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Foliage Plants for Zone 9 Containers and Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–March or September–November. Clara Curtis is among the most vigorous and spreading of the Chrysanthemum x rubellum species — divide clumps every 2–3 years to prevent crowding and maintain vigor. Pinch stem tips in spring and early summer to promote bushy, compact growth and abundant flowering; unpinched plants tend toward tall, floppy stems. In zone 9 it blooms September–November, providing fall color when most summer perennials are exhausted. The light fragrance of the daisy-like flowers is more pleasant than the sharp, camphor scent of many chrysanthemums.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Clara Curtis blooms equally well in partial shade, where the flower colors tend to be slightly richer and more saturated than in full sun conditions; Clara Curtis is a Chrysanthemum x rubellum species hybrid producing large (2–3 inch), single, daisy-form flowers in a warm soft pink to rose-pink color — distinctly different in both growth habit and flower form from the tight, pompom-flowered, potted mums of the florist trade. While florist mums are typically short-lived perennials requiring frequent replacement in Southern gardens, Clara Curtis is a genuinely robust, long-lived, spreading perennial that increases steadily in size over years, producing freely spreading clumps covered in the clear pink single daisy flowers from September through November. The single-flower form is more graceful and more naturalistically attractive than double mums, and the clear warm pink color works beautifully with the yellows, oranges, and burgundies of the fall palette. As a source of fall color in the perennial garden at a season when most summer plants are past peak, Clara Curtis is an irreplaceable addition to zone 9 gardens — blooming into November when frosts have begun and most summer color has ended.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Chrysanthemum 'Clara Curtis' Award of Garden Merit
Missouri Botanical Garden — Chrysanthemum x rubellum Plant Profile
North Carolina State University Extension — Hardy Chrysanthemums for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–May for summer bloom or August–September for fall bloom. Uproar Rose is a tall variety (24–30 inches) that may benefit from staking in exposed locations. For the longest bloom season, deadhead spent flowers regularly — though Uproar Rose blooms profusely with or without deadheading. Thin to 12 inches apart for best air circulation; zinnias are susceptible to powdery mildew when crowded, especially in humid conditions. The large, rose-pink flowers are outstanding cut flowers lasting 7–10 days in a vase.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — zinnias thrive in hot, sunny, moderately fertile conditions; Uproar Rose is an All-America Selections award-winning zinnia producing particularly large (4–5 inch), fully double, deep rose-pink flowers on tall, sturdy stems that are among the most impressive cut flowers available from direct-sown seed in the summer garden. The rich, saturated rose-pink color — more vibrant and deeper than pale pink varieties — maintains its intensity through summer heat, making Uproar Rose one of the most reliable large-flowered pink zinnias for the hot conditions of zone 9 summers. Zinnias in general are the quintessential summer annual for the Gulf South — they thrive in the heat that defeats many summer flowers, blooming continuously and heavily from planting through frost with minimal care. The large flowers are exceptional butterfly magnets, attracting swallowtails, fritillaries, monarchs, and sulphurs throughout the summer season, making zinnia plantings important pollinator habitat during the hottest months when many other nectar plants are stressed. Uproar Rose's combination of cut flower quality, striking color, heat tolerance, and pollinator value makes it one of the most multi-purpose zinnias available.
sources: All-America Selections — Zinnia Uproar Rose Award
Ball Horticultural — Zinnia Uproar Series Description
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Zinnias for Florida Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm (70°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May. Canary melons require warm to hot weather for full sugar development and are at their best in the extended heat of zone 9 summers. Unlike cantaloupe, canary melons do not slip from the vine when ripe — gauge ripeness by skin color turning from lime-green to brilliant chrome-yellow, slight softening at the blossom end, and the characteristic mild sweet fragrance. Cut from the vine when fully yellow and allow to rest at room temperature for 1–2 days for best flavor. The long shelf life (10–14 days at room temperature) compared to cantaloupes makes canary melons practical for home garden production.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-amended soil with consistent moisture during fruit development; Canary Melon (also called Spanish Melon or Yellow Melon) is an Inodorus-group muskmelon producing large (5–7 pound), oval to oblong fruits with a distinctive, intense, brilliant chrome-yellow skin at full ripeness — one of the most visually striking melons available, with the vivid yellow color making ripe fruits immediately identifiable in the garden and market. The flesh is white to pale ivory, very sweet, very juicy, with a mild, clean, faintly musky flavor that differs from cantaloupe's complex aromatic sweetness — canary melon's flavor is cleaner, lighter, and more delicate, making it the preferred melon for many people who find cantaloupe flavor too intense. The Inodorus (winter melon) classification means canary melons have firmer flesh and longer shelf life than cantaloupes — they don't 'slip' from the vine at ripeness, can be kept at room temperature for up to two weeks, and ship better than soft-fleshed types. Zone 9's extended warm season and summer heat are ideal for canary melon production, providing the long, hot growing period these melons need for full sugar development.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Melon Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Spanish and Canary Melons for Texas
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Melon Types for Florida Gardens
